papers.title: Physics-Guided Inductive Spatiotemporal Kriging for PM2.5 with Satellite Gradient Constraints
papers.authors: Shuo Wang, Mengfan Teng, Yun Cheng, Lothar Thiele, Olga Saukh, Shuangshuang He, Yuanting Zhang, Jiang Zhang, Gangfeng Zhang, Xingyuan Yuan, Jingfang Fan
papers.abstract: High-resolution mapping of fine particulate matter (PM2.5) is a cornerstone of sustainable urbanism but remains critically hindered by the spatial sparsity of ground monitoring networks. While traditional data-driven methods attempt to bridge this gap using satellite Aerosol Optical Depth (AOD), they often suffer from severe, non-random data missingness (e.g., due to cloud cover or nighttime) and inversion biases. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes the Spatiotemporal Physics-Guided Inference Network (SPIN), a novel framework designed for inductive spatiotemporal kriging. Unlike conventional approaches, SPIN synergistically integrates domain knowledge into deep learning by explicitly modeling physical advection and diffusion processes via parallel graph kernels. Crucially, we introduce a paradigm-shifting training strategy: rather than using error-prone AOD as a direct input, we repurpose it as a spatial gradient constraint within the loss function.

papers.title: A Bayesian Multisource Fusion Model for Spatiotemporal PM2.5 in an Urban Setting
papers.authors: Abi I. Riley, Marta Blangiardo, Frédéric B. Piel, Andrew Beddows, Sean Beevers, Gary W. Fuller, Paul Agnew, Monica Pirani
papers.abstract: Airborne particulate matter (PM2.5) is a major public health concern in urban environments, where population density and emission sources exacerbate exposure risks. We present a novel Bayesian spatiotemporal fusion model to estimate monthly PM2.5 concentrations over Greater London (2014-2019) at 1km resolution. The model integrates multiple PM2.5 data sources, including outputs from two atmospheric air quality dispersion models and predictive variables, such as vegetation and satellite aerosol optical depth, while explicitly modelling a latent spatiotemporal field. Spatial misalignment of the data is addressed through an upscaling approach to predict across the entire area.

papers.title: A comparison between geostatistical and machine learning models for spatio-temporal prediction of PM2.5 data
papers.abstract: Ambient air pollution poses significant health and environmental challenges. Exposure to high concentrations of PM2.5 have been linked to increased respiratory and cardiovascular hospital admissions. Traditional air quality monitoring systems such as EPA-certified stations provide limited spatial and temporal data. The advent of low-cost sensors has dramatically improved the granularity of air quality data. This study exploits the extensive data from PurpleAir sensors to assess and compare the effectiveness of various statistical and machine learning models in producing accurate hourly PM2.5 maps across California. We evaluate traditional geostatistical methods, including kriging and land use regression, against advanced machine learning approaches.

papers.title: China Regional 3km Downscaling Based on Residual Corrective Diffusion Model
papers.authors: Honglu Sun, Hao Jing, Zhixiang Dai, Sa Xiao, Wei Xue, Jian Sun, Qifeng Lu
papers.abstract: A fundamental challenge in numerical weather prediction is to efficiently produce high-resolution forecasts. A common solution is applying downscaling methods, which include dynamical downscaling and statistical downscaling, to the outputs of global models. This work focuses on statistical downscaling using a diffusion-based framework named CorrDiff. In order to generate the 3km forecasts for the China region, we apply our trained models to the 25km global grid forecasts of CMA-GFS, an operational global model of the China Meteorological Administration (CMA), and SFF, a data-driven deep learning-based weather model. CMA-MESO, a high-resolution regional model, is chosen as the baseline model.

papers.title: Generative Adversarial Models for Extreme Geospatial Downscaling
papers.abstract: Addressing the challenges of climate change requires accurate and high-resolution mapping of geospatial data, especially climate and weather variables. Many existing geospatial datasets, such as the gridded outputs of the state-of-the-art numerical climate models, are only available at very coarse spatial resolutions due to the model complexity and extremely high computational demand. Deep-learning-based methods, particularly generative adversarial networks (GANs) and their variants, have proved effective for refining natural images. This paper describes a conditional GAN-based stochastic geospatial downscaling method that can accommodate very high scaling factors. The method explicitly considers the uncertainty inherent to the downscaling process.

papers.title: Indoor PM2.5 forecasting and the association with outdoor air pollution: a modelling study based on sensor data in Australia
papers.authors: Wenhua Yu, Bahareh Nakisa, Seng W. Loke, Svetlana Stevanovic, Yuming Guo, Mohammad Naim Rastgoo
papers.abstract: Exposure to poor indoor air quality poses significant health risks, necessitating thorough assessment to mitigate associated dangers. This study aims to predict hourly indoor fine particulate matter (PM2.5) concentrations and investigate their correlation with outdoor PM2.5 levels across 24 distinct buildings in Australia. Indoor air quality data were gathered from 91 monitoring sensors in eight Australian cities spanning 2019 to 2022. Employing an innovative three-stage deep ensemble machine learning framework (DEML), comprising three base models and two meta-models, hourly indoor PM2.5 concentrations were predicted.

papers.title: Data level and decision level fusion of satellite multi-sensor AOD retrievals for improving PM2.5 estimations, a study on Tehran
papers.authors: Ali Mirzaei, Hossein Bagheri, Mehran Sattari
papers.abstract: One of the techniques for estimating the surface particle concentration with a diameter of fewer than 2.5 micrometers (PM2.5) is using aerosol optical depth (AOD) products. Different AOD products are retrieved from various satellite sensors, like MODIS and VIIRS, by various algorithms. Therefore, they do not have the same accuracy and spatial resolution. For the first time, the present study investigated the possibility of fusing AOD products from observations of MODIS and VIIRS sensors retrieved by Deep Blue and Dark Target algorithms to estimate PM2.5 more accurately. AOD products were fused by machine learning algorithms using different fusion strategies at two levels: the data level and the decision level.

papers.title: Air Quality PM2.5 Index Prediction Model Based on CNN-LSTM
papers.authors: Zicheng Guo, Shuqi Wu, Meixing Zhu, He Guandi
papers.abstract: With the intensification of global climate change, accurate prediction of air quality indicators, especially PM2.5 concentration, has become increasingly important in fields such as environmental protection, public health, and urban management. To address this, we propose an air quality PM2.5 index prediction model based on a hybrid CNN-LSTM architecture. The model effectively combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for local spatial feature extraction and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks for modeling temporal dependencies in time series data. Using a multivariate dataset collected from an industrial area in Beijing between 2010 and 2015, the model predicts the average PM2.5 concentration over 6-hour intervals.

papers.title: An Improved Multi-source Spatiotemporal Data Fusion Model Based on the Nearest Neighbor Grids for PM2.5 Concentration Interpolation and Prediction
papers.abstract: This paper proposes a novel multi-source spatiotemporal data fusion method via the nearest neighbor grids (MSF-NNG) to interpolate and predict PM2.5 concentration. The method divides the city into 1km grids and employs Cressman interpolation to fill missing values with observations from small stations, including PM2.5 concentrations, humidity, temperature, and wind speed. It finds neighbor grids based on similarity of atmospheric conditions.

papers.title: Estimation of PM2.5 Concentrations in China Using a Spatial Back Propagation Neural Network
papers.abstract: This study develops a spatial back-propagation neural network (S-BPNN) model for estimating spatial distribution of PM2.5 concentrations in China. The S-BPNN incorporates satellite observations of aerosol optical depth, meteorological data, emissions data, and auxiliary geographic parameters such as land use, NDVI, elevation, and population density. Principal components analysis is used for dimensionality reduction.

papers.title: Deep learning PM2.5 concentrations with bidirectional LSTM RNN
papers.abstract: This study develops a novel deep-learning-based spatiotemporal interpolation model using bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) for PM2.5 concentration interpolation. The model automatically accounts for both spatial and temporal hidden influencing factors. First application of bidirectional LSTM RNN in spatiotemporal interpolation of air pollutant concentrations. Evaluated using daily PM2.5 measurements in the southeast USA.

papers.title: Spatial calibration and PM2.5 mapping of low-cost air quality sensors
papers.abstract: This study proposes an efficient calibration and mapping approach for low-cost PM2.5 sensors based on real-time spatial models. The approach performs spatial calibration using reference-grade measurements at regulatory stations and investigates spatial varying patterns of calibrated data. Achieves R-square of 0.94 for explaining bias variability and reduces RMSE to 39% compared to non-spatial calibration.

papers.title: CleanAir: A deep-learning model for predicting daily PM2.5 concentration in response to emission reduction
papers.abstract: Air pollution remains a leading global health threat, with fine particulate matter (PM2.5) contributing to millions of premature deaths annually. Chemical transport models (CTMs) are essential tools for evaluating how emission controls improve air quality and save lives, but they are computationally intensive. Reduced form models accelerate simulations but sacrifice spatial-temporal granularity, accuracy, and flexibility. Here we present CleanAir, a deep-learning-based model developed as an efficient alternative to CTMs in simulating daily PM2.5 and its chemical compositions in response to precursor emission reductions at 36 km resolution. Built on a Residual Symmetric 3D U-Net architecture, CleanAir predicts PM2.5 concentration for a full year within 10 seconds on a single GPU.

papers.title: Deep-AIR: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Framework for Air Quality Modeling in Metropolitan Cities
papers.abstract: Air pollution has long been a critical environmental issue affecting public health worldwide. This paper proposes Deep-AIR, a hybrid CNN-LSTM deep learning framework for air quality modeling in metropolitan cities. The model combines Convolutional Neural Networks for extracting spatial features from monitoring station data and Long Short-Term Memory networks for capturing temporal dependencies. The framework is validated on real air quality data from multiple metropolitan areas, demonstrating superior performance compared to traditional statistical methods and single-model deep learning approaches.

papers.title: NeuroDDAF: Neural Dynamic Diffusion-Advection Fields with Evidential Fusion for Air Quality Forecasting
papers.abstract: This paper proposes NeuroDDAF, a novel neural network framework that models dynamic diffusion-advection fields with evidential fusion for air quality forecasting. The method incorporates physical diffusion and advection processes into a deep learning architecture and uses evidential theory to fuse multi-source data for uncertainty quantification. The model demonstrates strong performance in forecasting PM2.5 and other air quality parameters across multiple urban areas.

papers.title: TopoFlow: Physics-guided Neural Networks for high-resolution air quality prediction
papers.abstract: TopoFlow proposes physics-guided neural networks for high-resolution air quality prediction by integrating meteorological physics, topography, and wind field information with vision transformers. The model leverages CMAQ model outputs as physics-based features and combines them with satellite observations and topographic data through a physics-guided learning framework. The study demonstrates that incorporating physically-informed constraints significantly improves the accuracy and physical consistency of air quality predictions at high spatial resolution.

papers.title: GenDA: Generative Data Assimilation on Complex Urban Areas via Classifier-Free Diffusion Guidance
papers.abstract: GenDA presents a generative data assimilation framework for complex urban areas using classifier-free diffusion guidance. The method leverages diffusion models to assimilate multi-source observational data into urban air quality predictions, handling complex urban environments with heterogeneous data sources. The approach demonstrates superior performance in reconstructing complete spatiotemporal air quality fields from sparse and irregular monitoring data in urban settings.

papers.title: Diffusion-based Probabilistic Air Quality Forecasting with Mechanistic Insight
papers.abstract: This paper develops a diffusion-based probabilistic forecasting approach for air quality that incorporates mechanistic insight from atmospheric chemistry and physics. The method generates probabilistic predictions of air quality metrics including PM2.5 and ozone, providing uncertainty quantification alongside point predictions. The diffusion model is guided by domain knowledge from atmospheric science, resulting in physically consistent and probabilistically reliable forecasts.

papers.title: Advancing operational PM2.5 forecasting with dual deep neural networks (D-DNet)
papers.abstract: D-DNet introduces a dual deep neural network architecture for advancing operational PM2.5 forecasting. The model uses two cooperating deep networks to handle the different scales of PM2.5 variability and predict fine particulate matter concentrations at operational forecasting timescales. The approach demonstrates significant improvements over operational air quality forecasting systems and is validated on real-world monitoring data.

papers.title: EnsAI: An Emulator for Atmospheric Chemical Ensembles
papers.abstract: EnsAI presents an emulator for atmospheric chemical ensembles that leverages AI to approximate the behavior of ensemble atmospheric chemistry models including CMAQ. The framework generates ensemble predictions efficiently, capturing model uncertainty and ensemble diversity while requiring only a fraction of the computational cost of full chemical transport model ensembles. This enables rapid scenario analysis and uncertainty quantification for air quality applications.

papers.title: Zeeman: A Deep Learning Regional Atmospheric Chemistry Transport Model
papers.abstract: Zeeman proposes a deep learning regional atmospheric chemistry transport model that serves as a data-driven replacement for traditional CTMs like CMAQ. The model learns the complex physical and chemical processes governing atmospheric composition and PM2.5 formation from historical data, achieving comparable or superior accuracy to physics-based models while dramatically reducing computational requirements. The framework enables high-resolution, fast air quality simulations for large regions.

papers.title: Towards Kriging-informed Conditional Diffusion for Regional Sea-Level Data Downscaling
papers.authors: Subhankar Ghosh, Arun Sharma, Jayant Gupta, Aneesh Subramanian, Shashi Shekhar
papers.abstract: Given coarser-resolution projections from global climate models or satellite data, the downscaling problem aims to estimate finer-resolution regional climate data. Most downscaling methods fail to capture the spatial dependencies at finer scales. We propose a novel Kriging-informed Conditional Diffusion Probabilistic Model (Ki-CDPM) to capture spatial variability while preserving fine-scale features. Experimental results on climate data show that our proposed method is more accurate than state-of-the-art downscaling techniques.

papers.title: Generative downscaling of PDE solvers with physics-guided diffusion models
papers.abstract: Solving partial differential equations (PDEs) on fine spatio-temporal scales for high-fidelity solutions is critical. To speed up large-scale computations, a process known as downscaling is employed, which generates high-fidelity approximate solutions from their low-fidelity counterparts. We propose a novel Physics-Guided Diffusion Model (PGDM) for downscaling. Our model generates new high-fidelity approximations from any new low-fidelity inputs and refines them through fine-tuning aimed at minimizing physical discrepancies.

papers.title: Integrating Weather Station Data and Radar for Precipitation Nowcasting: SmaAt-fUsion and SmaAt-Krige-GNet
papers.authors: Jie Shi, Aleksej Cornelissen, Siamak Mehrkanoon
papers.abstract: Short-term precipitation nowcasting is essential. Many existing models fail to fully exploit the extensive atmospheric information available. The SmaAt-Krige-GNet model combines precipitation maps with weather station data processed using Kriging, a geo-statistical interpolation method, to generate variable-specific maps. These maps are then utilized in a dual-encoder architecture for multi-level data integration.

papers.title: Spatial Interpolation of Air Quality: A UK Case Study
papers.abstract: Air quality is an important aspect of both human health and climate change. This study analyzes spatial patterns of air pollutants (PM2.5, PM10, NO2) using IoT sensors for data collection. Various spatial interpolation methods including kriging techniques are evaluated for producing accurate air quality maps across multiple locations in a UK case study.

papers.title: Satellite-informed smart sensor placement framework for near-optimal PM2.5 monitoring in urban areas
papers.authors: Environmental Science and Pollution Research Authors
papers.abstract: This study proposes a novel algorithm that utilizes a multi-criteria optimization approach to identify optimal locations and distribution of PM2.5 monitoring sensors. The algorithm integrates geographical covariates (roads, population density, terrain elevation) and satellite observations of surface PM2.5. By applying Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II), optimal sensor placement is achieved for effective urban air quality monitoring.

papers.title: Spatial and temporal patterns of urban air pollution in Tehran with a focus on PM2.5 and associated pollutants
papers.abstract: This study analyzes the temporal dynamics of air quality monitoring stations (AQMSs) and the association among air pollutants, particularly PM2.5, in Tehran, Iran. Using time series clustering and the Copula model, data from 2019 to 2022 is analyzed to understand spatial and temporal patterns of urban air pollution.

papers.title: A novel CMAQ-CNN hybrid model to forecast hourly surface-ozone concentrations 14 days in advance
papers.abstract: This study develops a hybrid modeling system combining numerical modeling (CMAQ) and machine learning (CNN) for forecasting concentrations of surface ozone. The CNN model uses meteorology from WRF model, forecasted air quality from CMAQ, and previous 24-hour concentrations as inputs to predict 14-day hourly concentrations for 255 stations.

papers.title: A hyperlocal hybrid data fusion near-road PM2.5 and NO2 annual risk and environmental justice assessment across the United States
papers.authors: Alejandro Valencia, M. Serre, S. Arunachalam
papers.abstract: A hybrid data fusion approach combining CMAQ regional model with R-LINE local dispersion model using RAMP technique to estimate PM2.5 and NO2 exposures at census block resolution across the US.

papers.title: Spatio-temporal data fusion of censored threshold exceedances
papers.authors: M. Daniela Cuba, Craig Wilkie, Marian Scott, Daniela Castro-Camilo
papers.abstract: A Bayesian hierarchical data fusion framework rooted in extreme value theory for PM2.5 pollution using EAC4 reanalysis and in-situ observations, accounting for threshold exceedances and temporal structure.

papers.title: Spatial Transfer Learning for Estimating PM2.5 in Data-poor Regions
papers.authors: Shrey Gupta, Andreas Zufle, Yongbee Park, Jianzhao Bi, Suyash Gupta, Avani Wildani, Yang Liu
papers.abstract: Air pollution, especially particulate matter 2.5 (PM2.5), is a pressing concern for public health and is difficult to estimate in developing countries (data-poor regions) due to limited availability of ground monitoring stations. This study proposes spatial transfer learning methods to estimate PM2.5 in data-poor regions using knowledge from data-rich regions.

papers.title: Integrating mobile and fixed monitoring data for high-resolution PM2.5 mapping using machine learning
papers.abstract: This study integrates PM2.5 data from 320 taxi-mounted mobile low-cost sensors and 52 fixed monitoring stations to address the limitations of traditional fixed-site monitoring which lacks spatial coverage, while mobile low-cost sensors exhibit significant data instability. By employing machine learning methods, an appropriate mapping relationship was established between fixed and mobile monitoring concentration, achieving 500-meter spatial and 5-minute temporal resolutions.

papers.title: Spatio-Temporal Forecasting of PM2.5 via Spatial-Diffusion guided Encoder-Decoder Architecture
papers.authors: Malay Pandey, Vaishali Jain, Nimit Godhani, Sachchida Nand Tripathi, Piyush Rai
papers.abstract: In many problem settings that require spatio-temporal forecasting, the values in the time-series not only exhibit spatio-temporal correlations but are also influenced by spatial diffusion across locations. One such example is forecasting the concentration of fine particulate matter (PM2.5) in the atmosphere which is influenced by many complex factors, the most important ones being diffusion due to meteorological factors as well as transport across vast distances over a period of time. We present a novel Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Network architecture, that specifically captures these dependencies to forecast the PM2.5 concentration. Our model is based on an encoder-decoder architecture where the encoder and decoder parts leverage gated recurrent units (GRU) augmented with a graph neural network (TransformerConv) to account for spatial diffusion.

papers.title: Augmenting Ground-Level PM2.5 Prediction via Kriging-Based Pseudo-Label Generation
papers.authors: Lei Duan, Ziyang Jiang, David Carlson
papers.abstract: Fusing abundant satellite data with sparse ground measurements constitutes a major challenge in climate modeling. To address this, we propose a strategy to augment the training dataset by introducing unlabeled satellite images paired with pseudo-labels generated through a spatial interpolation technique known as ordinary kriging, thereby making full use of the available satellite data resources. We show that the proposed data augmentation strategy helps enhance the performance of the state-of-the-art convolutional neural network-random forest (CNN-RF) model by a reasonable amount, resulting in a noteworthy improvement in spatial correlation and a reduction in prediction error.
